company.name: Character.AI
company.tagline: From a $1B chatbot unicorn to a $2.7B reverse-acquihire — and the consumer AI company that had to be rebuilt without its founders
company.summary: Character.AI compressed three full company arcs into 36 months: a viral consumer launch with engagement metrics no one could match, a safety crisis triggered by a teen suicide, and a one-of-a-kind Google deal that returned its co-founders to Mountain View while leaving the company technically alive. The shape of this story is engagement-rich and revenue-light, with a forced restructuring that rewrote what 'consumer AI' could even mean.

events.title: Character Technologies founded
events.description: Noam Shazeer (Transformer co-author) and Daniel De Freitas (LaMDA / Meena lead) leave Google after the company refuses to ship their chatbot publicly. They incorporate Character Technologies in Delaware to build the consumer version themselves.

events.title: Seed round (~$43M)
events.description: Early seed/pre-Series A capital from Elad Gil, SV Angel, Nat Friedman, A Capital. Stealth-mode build.

events.title: Public beta launch
events.description: Character.AI opens to the public — free, no waitlist. Users can create and chat with custom AI characters. ChatGPT had not launched yet (still 11 weeks away). Hundreds of thousands of conversations within weeks.

events.title: Series A $150M / $1B
events.description: Led by Andreessen Horowitz (Sarah Wang to the board), with Nat Friedman, Elad Gil, SV Angel, A Capital. Unicorn status six months after launch — driven by engagement, not revenue.

events.title: iOS + Android app — 1.7M installs in week one
events.description: Mobile launch. 700K Android installs in 48 hours, 1.7M total in under a week. Outpaces Netflix, Disney+, Prime Video on the entertainment chart. 99% organic.

events.title: c.ai+ subscription launches at $9.99/mo
events.description: First monetization layer: priority access during peak load, faster responses, early features. Community reaction mixed — first signal of the engagement-vs-monetization tension.

events.title: Sewell Setzer III dies by suicide
events.description: A 14-year-old in Florida dies by suicide. The case becomes public eight months later, when his mother's lawsuit (Garcia v. Character Technologies) alleges the death followed months of conversations with a Game of Thrones-themed character on the platform.

events.title: Character Voice ships
events.description: AI voices in 1:1 chat — initially gated to c.ai+, then opened to all users. The first multimodal step beyond text.

events.title: Character Calls — phone-call mode
events.description: Two-way voice conversations in 9+ languages. The pre-launch test logged 20M calls from 3M users. A bigger product leap than text → voice.

events.title: Google deal — $2.7B reverse acquihire
events.description: Google pays ~$2.7B for a non-exclusive license to Character.AI's technology, brings Noam Shazeer + Daniel De Freitas + ~30 researchers back to Google DeepMind. Character.AI continues operating as an independent company. Investors are bought out at the implied valuation; remaining equity passes to employees.

events.title: Dominic Perella named interim CEO
events.description: Former general counsel takes over the day the Google deal closes. Public message: Character.AI exits the foundation-model race and concentrates on the consumer product.

events.title: Erin Teague (ex-YouTube) joins as CPO
events.description: First major non-founder hire post-deal. Signal that the company is rebuilding for consumer entertainment, not foundation-model competition.

events.title: Garcia v. Character Technologies filed
events.description: Megan Garcia files a wrongful-death suit in Florida federal court (case docketed Oct 23). Strict product liability, negligence, deceptive trade practices. The case names Character.AI, Shazeer, De Freitas, and Google. Wall-to-wall coverage in NYT, CNN, NBC, Washington Post.

events.title: Texas lawsuit — second teen safety case
events.description: Filed in eastern Texas federal court. Two minors, including a 17-year-old with autism whose chatbot allegedly suggested it 'understood' children killing parents. Plaintiffs seek to force the platform offline. Texas AG opens its own investigation three days later.

events.title: Teen safety model + chat-time caps
events.description: Dedicated under-18 model, suicide-prevention pop-ups, two-hour daily caps for minors, in-house age-verification work. The most aggressive consumer-AI safety reset to date — built under legal duress.

events.title: AvatarFX ships
events.description: Image + voice → animated lifelike video character. DiT-based diffusion, Character.AI proprietary TTS. The first product launch where the new company defines its own technical edge — explicitly entertainment, not assistant.

events.title: Social feed + community video features
events.description: Platform pivots from 1:1 chat to creator-feed. Brands like Yelp, Webtoon start placing ads in the feed — first ad-revenue layer.

events.title: Karandeep Anand named CEO
events.description: Ex-Brex president, ex-Meta VP of Business Products, ex-Microsoft. Nine months on the board first. The first permanent CEO of the post-Google company. Stated priorities: memory, model quality, less-overbearing safety filters, monetization.

events.title: TechCrunch Disrupt — public reset narrative
events.description: Anand's first big-stage interview as CEO. Frames Character.AI as 'AI entertainment,' not assistant or companion. Reports Scenes & Streams, social feed engagement holding despite MAU decline.

events.title: Under-18 chat ban announced
events.description: Open-ended chat for users under 18 deprecated by November 25. Minors retained access only to creative tools (AvatarFX, Imagine Chat, stories, streams). A core demographic — over half the visitor base sat in 18–24 — voluntarily walled off.

events.title: Garcia + Texas lawsuits settled
events.description: Court filing discloses agreements with the Garcia family and additional plaintiffs. Google co-defendant in the settlement. Terms not disclosed.

platforms.name: TikTok
platforms.description: TikTok was where Character.AI grew. Cosplay-style 'I made my favorite anime character' demo videos compounded for two years. The company never ran an account that mattered — the platform's algorithm did the distribution work. #characterai stacked billions of views by mid-2024.
platforms.catalyst: No single moment. A two-year wave of teen and Gen-Z creators posting screen-recorded conversations with Loki, Bakugo, and other fandom characters. The single biggest reason engagement metrics ran ahead of every other consumer AI product.
platforms.whenItWorks: When the product naturally turns into a screen-recordable moment — especially fandom or roleplay output that travels as content on its own
platforms.whenItDoesnt: If you try to seed it with brand creators. The audience reads sponsored content instantly, and the algorithm punishes it

platforms.name: Reddit
platforms.role: Power-user habitat and crisis ground zero
platforms.description: r/CharacterAI is one of the largest consumer-AI subreddits. Through 2023–2024 it was the de facto support, advocacy, and feedback channel — where filter changes, model regressions, and feature launches got triaged in real time. Also where the safety-feature backlash organized in late 2024.
platforms.catalyst: The 'filter wars' threads (2023–2024) where the community pushed back on safety filters that softened character behavior. Each adjustment surfaced in r/CharacterAI within hours and shaped the next product cycle.
platforms.whenItWorks: When you have a critical-mass user base willing to evangelize and complain. Reddit is where consumer AI products learn what's actually breaking
platforms.whenItDoesnt: If the company tries to control the narrative. The community detects it and trust collapses

platforms.name: YouTube
platforms.description: YouTube was where 'How to use Character.AI' tutorials and roleplay-style storytelling videos lived. Less explosive than TikTok per video, but longer half-life. The platform also carried podcast appearances by Shazeer that built investor and tech-press credibility.
platforms.catalyst: Noam Shazeer's No Priors and Cognitive Revolution appearances (2023–2024) put the founder narrative — Transformer co-author, LaMDA frustration, consumer AI bet — into the long-form record investors actually consume.
platforms.whenItWorks: When the product produces watchable artifacts — roleplay, voice calls, video — and the founder has a real technical narrative
platforms.whenItDoesnt: If your only assets are 30-second clips. Those belong on TikTok or Shorts, not main YouTube

platforms.name: Instagram
platforms.description: Reels picked up the second wave of TikTok content — the same cosplay and roleplay clips, slightly older audience. Useful but secondary to TikTok and Reddit. Became more important once AvatarFX launched and content became visually richer.
platforms.catalyst: AvatarFX launch (April 2025) — the first time Character.AI had visual output that was genuinely Instagram-native. Animated character clips travelled well in Reels.
platforms.whenItWorks: When you already have video output traveling on TikTok. Reels is the cheap second-platform port
platforms.whenItDoesnt: If you start there. Instagram rewards platform-native creators, not B2C-tool brand pages

platforms.name: X (Twitter)
platforms.description: X was never the consumer growth channel — the user base lives on TikTok and Reddit, not in tech-Twitter. But X carried the founder credibility for fundraising: Noam Shazeer's Transformer pedigree and the Series A announcement traveled through the AI-Twitter network and shaped investor mind-share.
platforms.catalyst: March 2023 Series A — a16z and Sarah Wang amplified the announcement across investor X. The 'Transformer co-author starts consumer AI' frame was an X-native story.
platforms.whenItWorks: For investor-class signal and AI-research credibility. Your founders matter more than your account here
platforms.whenItDoesnt: As a consumer acquisition channel for a Gen-Z product. The audience is wrong

platforms.name: Hacker News
platforms.description: HN front-paged the 2022 public launch and again the 2024 Google deal — both natural fits for an audience that recognized Shazeer's name. Less relevant during the consumer growth years (HN's audience isn't roleplaying with anime characters) but load-bearing at the bookends of the story.
platforms.catalyst: August 2024 Google deal — front-page HN with hundreds of comments dissecting the reverse-acquihire structure and what it implied for Anthropic / Inflection / future deals.
platforms.whenItWorks: When your story has a structural-novelty hook (founder pedigree, deal structure, technical novelty)
platforms.whenItDoesnt: Don't post incremental product news. HN punishes 'we shipped a feature' framing for a B2C entertainment product
